content.text: The cut-line is the boundary that divides what ships in the current release train from what is deferred to the next one. It is a moment in time (scope freeze) combined with a set of criteria (readiness, risk, eval pass rate) that together determine which items are above the line (in) and which are below it (deferred). Once the cut-line is enforced, the train's scope is fixed: no new work enters regardless of how close to complete it appears.

The cut-line process is the set of steps that lead up to, execute, and document that decision. It begins with scope freeze — typically at the start of week two of a two-week train cycle — and continues through a risk assessment, a stakeholder communication, and a formal cut decision record that documents what is in, what is out, and why. The process exists to make the scope boundary legible, enforceable, and reproducible across every train cycle.

For agentic AI systems, the cut-line carries additional weight because 'readiness' is multidimensional. A feature that is code-complete may still fail the cut if its eval pass rate on task-specific golden tests is below the threshold, if the model version it was developed against has been updated since development began, or if a dependent tool integration has not been validated in the staging environment. The cut process for an AI system must account for all of these dimensions simultaneously.

Without a cut-line, scope expands to fill available time. Engineers add one more thing because the release date is still a week away. Product managers slip in a high-priority request that 'only needs a small change.' Each addition is individually defensible and collectively produces a large, complex, poorly-tested release. Large releases fail in production more often, take longer to debug, and are harder to roll back.

The cut-line prevents this by making scope a first-class, time-bounded decision. Once it is cut, the scope is known, the risk is assessable, and the stabilisation period can begin with confidence that nothing new will destabilise it. For AI systems, where a single prompt change or tool integration addition can produce subtle quality regressions detectable only through eval runs, the discipline of cutting scope before stabilisation is especially important.

Deferred items are not lost. They are explicitly recorded with a reason for deferral and automatically become candidates for the next train's scope meeting. This creates a virtuous cycle: teams that consistently defer work learn to start earlier in the cycle so that their features are above the cut-line when it arrives.
After the cut-line, every stakeholder knows exactly what is and is not in the release. No surprises on deploy day.
Once the scope is fixed, the stabilisation team can work against a known change set. Regression bugs introduced during stabilisation are attributable to a known set of commits.
Deferred features reduce the change surface of each release, which reduces the number of things that can go wrong in production.
The cut decision log records why each item was included or deferred, providing context for post-mortems and future scope discussions.
Engineers who miss the cut-line consistently learn to begin work at the start of the sprint rather than mid-week, improving flow.
The cut decision notification tells stakeholders definitively what they will and will not get in the upcoming release, allowing them to plan downstream work accordingly.
A feature that is 95% complete at the cut-line will be deferred for an entire train cycle. This is genuinely frustrating for the engineer who built it, and it requires explaining to stakeholders.
Product leaders will sometimes push hard to include high-business-value features after the cut. Resisting this pressure requires discipline and organisational support for the train model.

A bug found in a feature after the cut-line can only be fixed (not deferred) if the feature is already in the train. Fixing a bug in an in-train feature during stabilisation is allowed; adding a new feature is not.
AI eval scoring is not binary.

A feature with an eval pass rate of 89% against a 90% threshold is very close to passing. Deciding whether to defer it, do a partial fix in stabilisation, or lower the threshold for this train requires judgment that resists pure process automation.
The cut-line process runs in four steps. In step one —

— the engineering lead announces that no new work will be accepted into the train. All open pull requests are reviewed: only those already approved and passing CI are eligible for merging after freeze. New PRs are tagged for the next train.

— every feature that has been merged runs through the full golden-set eval. Features below the eval threshold are flagged. The team decides whether a targeted fix can bring the eval score above threshold within the stabilisation window, or whether the feature must be reverted from the train.

— each in-train feature is scored for deployment risk: complexity of the change, surface area affected, presence of database migrations, and dependency on external integrations. High-risk features that cannot be mitigated (e.g. by a feature flag) are candidates for deferral.

— the final list of what is in and what is out is written to the cut decision log. Stakeholders receive a structured notification: what shipped, what was deferred, and the reason for each deferral. The deferred list feeds directly into the next train's scope planning meeting.

attrs.alt: Cut-line process: a Feature Backlog on the left contains 7 items with readiness indicators. A vertical CUT LINE divides it from Decision Criteria (eval pass rate, feature readiness, risk, late requests, feature flags). Arrows route green items to an IN This Train box and red/amber items to a DEFERRED box. The IN items flow to Train Departs to Production. A Cut Decision Log at the bottom captures all decisions.

content.text: Features A, B, and Bug Fix C meet all criteria and cross the cut-line into the train. Features D (eval below threshold), E (incomplete review), F (high risk), and G (late arrival) are deferred. Each deferral is documented in the cut decision log with a specific reason, and the log becomes the input to the next train's scope planning meeting.
Apply the cut-line process to every train cycle, unconditionally.
It becomes most valuable precisely when there is pressure to skip it (tight timeline, high-value feature close to done).
Use a hard eval threshold as a cut criterion
for AI system features. A feature that does not pass its eval does not ship, regardless of code completeness.
Use feature flags to keep nearly-ready features in the train
without enabling them for users. The feature ships but is off by default, allowing the team to enable it incrementally after validating in production.
Do not revert the cut-line for a single stakeholder's request
after it has been communicated. Once stakeholders know the cut-line date is fixed, they stop making late requests — but only after they learn, through experience, that the requests do not succeed.
Reserve post-cut merges for bug fixes only.
A post-cut bug fix must fix a defect in existing in-train code, not add new functionality. Enforce this distinction at the PR review level.
Set the cut-line date in the train calendar and announce it at the start of the cycle. Publish it in the team's planning document, sprint board, and Slack channel.
Tag every feature with its target train version (GitHub label, Jira Fix Version, Linear cycle). This creates the official in-train list that is checked at cut time.
At the cut-line, create a release branch:
. Only approved bug-fix PRs merge into this branch after the cut. Feature PRs target the next train's branch.
Run the full eval suite on the release branch immediately after the cut-line. Flag any feature whose eval score is below threshold for potential revert.

Conduct the risk assessment. Score each in-train feature on a simple 1–3 scale: 1 = low risk (config change, well-tested), 2 = medium risk (new code path, integration updated), 3 = high risk (schema migration, external API change, new model). Features scoring 3 without a mitigation plan (feature flag, staged rollout) are candidates for deferral.
Write the cut decision log: a YAML or Markdown file committed to the repo listing every feature with its decision (IN or DEFERRED) and reason.
Send the stakeholder notification. Use a template that lists: release version, departure date, features IN, features DEFERRED with reasons, known limitations of this release.

# release/cut-decisions-v1.4.0.yaml
train: v1.4.0
cut_line_date: 2025-07-28
cut_decision_author: eng-lead

in_train:
  - id: AGENT-142
    title: "Multi-step tool chaining for file search"
    eval_pass_rate: 0.95
    risk: low
    reason: "Code complete, eval passing, no migrations"

  - id: AGENT-148
    title: "Rate limit retry with exponential backoff"
    eval_pass_rate: 0.97
    risk: low
    reason: "Small, well-tested, backward compatible"

  - id: BUGFIX-29
    title: "Fix tool result truncation at 4096 tokens"
    eval_pass_rate: null
    risk: low
    reason: "P1 bug fix, regression tested, no new surface"

deferred:
  - id: AGENT-151
    title: "Parallel tool execution (experimental)"
    eval_pass_rate: 0.72
    risk: high
    reason: "Eval below 90% threshold; needs prompt tuning. Target: v1.5.0"
    target_train: v1.5.0

  - id: AGENT-155
    title: "Memory summarisation module"
    eval_pass_rate: null
    risk: medium
    reason: "PR not approved at cut-line. Target: v1.5.0"
    target_train: v1.5.0

  - id: AGENT-160
    title: "GPT-4o fallback when Anthropic unavailable"
    eval_pass_rate: null
    risk: high
    reason: "Late request received after cut-line. Target: v1.5.0"
    target_train: v1.5.0
GitHub Actions: Enforce Cut-Line (No Feature PRs After Freeze)

# .github/workflows/cut-line-guard.yml
# Blocks merges of feature PRs into a release branch after code freeze
name: Cut-line Guard

on:
  pull_request:
    branches:
      - 'release/**'

jobs:
  check-pr-type:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Reject feature PRs after code freeze
        run: |
          LABELS=$(echo '${{ toJSON(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name) }}')
          if echo "$LABELS" | grep -q '"feature"'; then
            echo "ERROR: Feature PRs cannot merge into a release branch after code freeze."
            echo "Target the next train branch instead, or request a feature-flag exception."
            exit 1
          fi
          if echo "$LABELS" | grep -q '"bugfix"\|"hotfix"'; then
            echo "Bug fix or hotfix PR: allowed on release branch."
            exit 0
          fi
          echo "ERROR: PR must be labelled 'bugfix' or 'hotfix' to merge into release branch."
          exit 1

# scripts/send_cut_notification.py
# Generates and sends the cut decision notification to stakeholders

import yaml
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime

def generate_notification(cut_log_path: str) -> str:
    log = yaml.safe_load(Path(cut_log_path).read_text())
    train = log["train"]
    lines = [
        f"Release {train} — Cut Decision Summary",
        f"Scope freeze: {log['cut_line_date']}",
        "",
        "IN THIS RELEASE:",
    ]
    for item in log["in_train"]:
        lines.append(f"  • [{item['id']}] {item['title']}")

    lines.append("")
    lines.append("DEFERRED TO NEXT TRAIN:")
    for item in log["deferred"]:
        lines.append(
            f"  • [{item['id']}] {item['title']}\n"
            f"    Reason: {item['reason']}\n"
            f"    Target: {item['target_train']}"
        )

    lines.append("")
    lines.append("Questions? Contact the release engineering team.")
    return "\n".join(lines)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    notification = generate_notification("release/cut-decisions-v1.4.0.yaml")
    print(notification)
    # In production: send via email or Slack webhook

A team running a bi-weekly release train for an agentic research assistant encountered a recurring pattern: the feature for parallel tool execution kept appearing in train backlogs but never shipping. Investigation revealed the feature's eval pass rate was consistently around 72–78% — below the 90% threshold — because the prompt coordination logic for parallel calls produced malformed tool result merging in roughly one in four attempts.

Rather than accepting the feature at the lower pass rate or indefinitely deferring it, the team used the cut decision log to make the deferral explicit and attached a clear remediation criterion: the feature would enter the train when and only when eval pass rate exceeded 90% on the parallel-tool golden set. The responsible engineer used the deferral as motivation to redesign the result-merging logic. Three train cycles later, the feature shipped with a 94% eval pass rate. The documented deferral history gave the go/no-go meeting clear evidence that the quality issue was genuinely resolved rather than masked.
Think of the cut-line as a

. An airline sets a gate-close time before departure. Passengers who arrive before gate close board. Passengers who arrive after do not board that flight, regardless of how close they are to the gate, how important their meeting is at the destination, or how long they have been waiting in the airport. The gate closes because the plane has a departure slot and cannot hold. The passengers who missed the gate take the next flight — they are inconvenienced, not stranded. The discipline of gate closing is what makes the departure schedule reliable for the thousands of passengers who did board on time.

The most damaging pattern is an organisation where every cut is met with a request for an exception, and exceptions are granted often enough that stakeholders learn to make them. The cut-line becomes a suggestion rather than a rule. The fix is consistency: hold the cut-line for three consecutive trains without exceptions, and the exception requests stop coming because stakeholders learn they will not succeed.

Verbal cut decisions made in a meeting and not committed to a written log are forgotten within a week. The next train's scope planning meeting has no record of why certain items were deferred, leading to the same items being proposed again without awareness of the prior evaluation. Always write the cut decision log and commit it to the repository.

When an in-train feature fails its eval during stabilisation, the temptation is to do a quick fix rather than revert. Quick fixes under time pressure are a leading cause of production incidents. If a fix cannot be completed, reviewed, and re-evaluated within the stabilisation window with confidence, the feature should be reverted from the train — even if it was code-complete before the cut-line.
Confusing Hotfixes with Post-Cut Feature Additions

A 'small improvement that addresses user feedback' is a feature addition, not a hotfix, even if it is small. Gate post-cut merges strictly on the type of change (defect fix in existing code), not on size. The enforcement mechanism is the GitHub Actions cut-line guard that rejects PRs without a
Use a CI check that blocks feature PRs from merging into the release branch after freeze. Automation makes the rule impersonal — it is the pipeline that refuses, not a specific engineer.
Write the cut decision log to the repository.
to the repo so that it is versioned, reviewable, and searchable.
Use feature flags as an alternative to hard deferral.
A feature that is code-complete but risky can ship behind a flag (off by default) rather than being deferred. This satisfies the engineer while protecting production.
Define deferral criteria at the start of the cycle.

The cut decision should not be a surprise. During scope planning, agree on the criteria that will be used at the cut-line (eval threshold, risk score, completion definition). Engineers know from day one what they need to achieve.
Treat deferred items as first-class backlog.
The next train's scope planning meeting opens with the deferred list from the previous train. Items should not disappear from the backlog just because they were deferred.

Send the cut notification to stakeholders within 24 hours of the cut decision. Stakeholders who learn about deferrals on deploy day are more frustrated than those who learned about them a week earlier.

The cut-line process is a coordination mechanism with no direct compute cost. Its performance characteristics are organisational: how quickly can the team run evals, assess risk, and make decisions? The bottleneck is usually the eval run itself.
The cut-line eval is a full golden-set run. Use job parallelism to complete it in 15–30 minutes rather than hours.

Track risk scores per PR during the development week so that the cut decision meeting can move quickly. A PR with a pre-assigned risk score of 1 does not need discussion; only 3-scored PRs need debate.
Generate the cut notification from the cut decision log YAML and send it via a script. Reduce the manual effort of the cut-line to reviewing and approving the generated output.
. Branch protection rules on the release branch, combined with a CI check, block unlabelled or feature-labelled PRs from merging after freeze.
Assign every in-train issue to a milestone named after the release version. The milestone progress bar shows, in real time, how many items are closed vs. open.
Eval tracking platforms that score each feature branch's eval pass rate and compare it against the release baseline, making the include/defer decision data-driven.
Feature flag platforms that let borderline-ready features ship in the train with the flag off, then be gradually enabled in production without a new release.
(GitHub Action) automatically draft release notes from merged PR titles and labels, reducing the manual effort of writing the cut notification.

The cut-line process directly improves release reliability by keeping each release's change surface small and well-understood. Every feature that is deferred is a potential source of production incidents that the current release avoids. For AI systems, where emergent interactions between prompt changes, model updates, and tool integrations can produce surprising quality regressions, the discipline of cutting scope before stabilisation is a quality control mechanism as much as a scheduling one.
Any PR that merges into the release branch after freeze must be reviewed by the release lead, not just by the usual PR reviewer. This ensures a second pair of eyes on every post-cut change.
Re-run evals after every post-cut bug fix.
A bug fix merged after the cut-line can introduce regressions. Run the full eval suite after each post-cut merge, not just at the cut-line.
Keep the cut decision log in version control.
The log is the audit trail for what shipped and why. It is the first document referenced in a production post-mortem.
The cut-line is a fixed scope boundary: everything above ships in the current train; everything below is deferred to the next, without exception after the freeze.
The cut-line process runs in four steps: scope freeze, eval pass check, risk assessment, and cut decision with stakeholder communication.
For AI system trains, eval pass rate is a hard inclusion criterion — a feature below the eval threshold does not ship regardless of code completeness.
All cut decisions — IN and DEFERRED — must be documented in a written cut decision log committed to the repository, with specific reasons for each decision.
Feature flags are the preferred alternative to hard deferral for nearly-ready features: ship dark, enable incrementally, without delaying the train.
Automate post-cut enforcement with a CI check that blocks feature PRs from merging into the release branch; make the rule impersonal so it does not depend on individual engineers to enforce it.
Deferred items must be first-class citizens in the next train's scope planning meeting — deferral is a scheduling decision, not abandonment.
